chunk_summary: Guidelines for preventing and managing vitamin D toxicity, including dosing clarity, monitoring, and treatment of hypercalcemia, with a focus on pediatric care in India.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical features and diagnostic criteria for vitamin D toxicity.
Learn strategies to prevent vitamin D toxicity, including proper dosing and caregiver education.
Identify appropriate management steps for suspected or confirmed vitamin D toxicity.
Recognize the importance of follow-up and reassessment in children receiving therapeutic vitamin D.
key_facts: Vitamin D toxicity is defined by 25(OH)D levels >100 ng/mL, often with hypercalcemia and/or hypercalciuria.
High-strength preparations (e.g., 60,000 IU sachets) increase the risk of dosing errors in India.
Clinical features include nausea, vomiting, constipation, lethargy, polyuria, and nephrocalcinosis in severe cases.
Avoid 'stoss' therapy in young infants unless under specialist supervision due to risk of prolonged hypercalcemia.
Management includes stopping vitamin D and calcium, assessing hydration, and treating hypercalcemia with hydration, diuretics, or glucocorticoids if severe.
Children on therapeutic vitamin D should have planned reassessment to review symptoms and adjust dosing.
reference_quote: Toxicity is defined by very high 25(OH)D levels (often >100 ng/mL) with hypercalcemia and/or hypercalciuria.
document_key_topics: Follow-up for therapeutic vitamin D use
